Q1: I tried to be really disciplined with my study routine, dedicating about three hours every day to coursework. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while taking notes, sometimes replaying sections multiple times to really absorb the material. I kept a basic schedule on my phone to track what needed to be done each week. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when and where I studied to make the most of my time.
Q2: I tried to keep things regular, but honestly my pattern shifted a lot based on deadlines and how anxious I was feeling about upcoming exams. Early in the week I'd stick to my schedule, but as assignments approached, I'd end up studying late into the night. The anxiety made it hard to maintain consistency because sometimes I'd study extra hours just to feel less scared about falling behind, even when I'd already covered the material.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new announcements or to see if any discussion posts had responses. I also went in specifically to watch the lecture videos since those were essential for how I learn best. Sometimes the fear of missing something important drove me to log in even when I wasn't planning to study. It became almost compulsive during stressful weeks.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine, like right after I woke up I'd scroll through to see what was new. It happened naturally because I was taking so many online courses and needed to stay on top of everything. The peer support group I connected with also encouraged regular check-ins, which helped normalize it. Even though I only logged in a few times a week formally, I was mentally always thinking about what was happening there.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening and hearing explanations. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers helped me understand concepts from different angles. I spent over three hours watching videos because I could pause, rewind, and really process the information at my own pace. The peer interactions especially helped when I was feeling scared about the material.
Q6: I tended to skip over reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources because they just don't stick for me the way audio does. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd skim them quickly or ignore them entirely. With limited internet access sometimes, I had to prioritize what I downloaded, so optional readings got cut first. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, I just couldn't absorb information that way efficiently.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could listen to it or discuss it with peers, that made it feel more valuable to my learning style. I also valued activities where I felt like I was making progress, even small wins helped with my anxiety. When something felt disconnected from the actual assessments, it was harder to justify spending time on it given how stretched I already was.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. Required ones created this pressure that actually helped me focus despite my anxiety, while optional ones left me paralyzed about whether to invest time in them. Sometimes I'd do optional discussion posts because connecting with peers helped calm my nerves. But when stress was high, optional stuff got pushed aside even if I knew it might help.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone during bus rides or while doing chores at home. I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back, which really helped cement the information. I also studied with peers in person when we could meet up, quizzing each other verbally. A lot of my actual learning happened through these audio-based methods that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be walking around with earbuds in, listening to downloaded lectures or my own recorded summaries. I'd spend hours in study groups where we'd talk through problems together, but none of that registered anywhere. Sometimes I'd be lying in bed at night mentally reviewing what I'd learned, processing everything through repetition. The LMS only saw when I clicked things, not when I was actually thinking and learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video once, then watch it three or four more times offline over the following days. With my limited resources and sometimes spotty internet, downloading and studying offline was essential. I'd take audio notes while watching the first time, then review those notes repeatedly without needing to access the platform again. One download could fuel a whole week of studying for me.
Q12: Around exam time, my anxiety spiked so badly that I sometimes couldn't focus even when I was trying to study. There were weeks when I felt genuinely scared about failing, and that fear made it harder to actually engage with new material. When multiple deadlines converged, I'd feel paralyzed and spend time worrying instead of working. The peer support helped me push through, but those moments were really dark.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from studying so many hours, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities at home, since I don't have many resources, meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other obligations. The fatigue combined with anxiety created this fog where I was technically present but not really learning. Confusion about complex topics made me avoid certain modules entirely sometimes.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd watch a video but my mind was racing with worry about other assignments or life stuff. I'd reach the end and realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too anxious. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them complete, hoping I'd revisit them later with a clearer head. Being scared about my performance made it hard to be fully present even when I was trying.
Q15: I avoided exam review materials sometimes because looking at them made my anxiety worse. There were practice problems I knew I should do, but the fear of getting them wrong and confirming I didn't understand the material was paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's frustrating because I knew avoidance was making things worse, but the fear was overwhelming.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They created intense anxiety spikes, but they also forced me to actually complete things I might have otherwise avoided. I'd often do my best work right before deadlines because the pressure cut through the fear and made me focus. My assignment completion rate was perfect because deadlines gave me no choice but to push through the anxiety and submit something.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me, though sometimes it increased my anxiety if it was critical. Positive feedback from peers in discussions gave me confidence to participate more. When instructors gave constructive comments, I'd listen to my recordings of the lectures again to address the gaps they identified. The peer support intervention specifically helped me process feedback without spiraling into fear about my abilities.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me stay organized despite taking fifteen online courses. Having regular deadlines kept me accountable, which I needed given my tendency to get paralyzed by anxiety. The discussion requirements pushed me to interact with peers, which ended up being really valuable for my mental state. But the structure also felt overwhelming sometimes with so much content to cover.
Q19: Having video lectures made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums being active helped me feel less alone in struggling with the material. What made it harder was when important information was only in text format, or when modules were released all at once instead of gradually. The exam format was particularly challenging because my understanding didn't translate well to written test performance.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard but struggling to understand the material, so they keep reviewing without being able to produce quality work. Or maybe anxiety makes them check and recheck things without actually completing submissions. It could also be someone like me who engages a lot with discussions and videos but then freezes up when it comes to high-stakes assessments. Activity doesn't equal understanding or the ability to demonstrate that understanding.
Q21: That makes sense if someone learns primarily through offline methods or has a different learning style. Someone might download everything once and study extensively without generating more log data. Or they might be naturally good at the subject and not need as much platform interaction. For me, my high attendance and completion rates didn't save my exam scores, so the relationship between activity and performance isn't straightforward.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone is most active, how often they access materials, and whether they complete required tasks. But they miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the clicks, and all the offline learning. They can't capture that I listened to a downloaded lecture five times or that I was crying from anxiety while watching a video. The numbers tell a story, but it's not the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just going through the motions. It's about caring enough to keep pushing even when you're scared or struggling. For me, engagement included reaching out to peers when I was confused, listening to lectures multiple times, and showing up consistently even when anxiety made me want to hide. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always show up in metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much courage it took for me to keep going despite high anxiety and the fear of failure. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline with limited resources, or how peer support literally kept me in the course when I wanted to give up. It can't show that I completed every single assignment while terrified, or that my exam score doesn't reflect my actual understanding of the material. My resilience is invisible to the data.
